serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of abalone sauce seafood rice is approximately 350 g, comprising a base of steamed white rice topped with mixed seafood and abalone sauce dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Abalone Sauce Seafood Rice 鲍汁海鲜捞饭 — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Abalone Sauce Seafood Rice (鲍汁海鲜捞饭), cr_id G00010916. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for abalone sauce seafood rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 72 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white jasmine rice, which has a well-established GI of approximately 68–72 in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008; Brand-Miller et al.). The seafood and sauce components contribute negligible carbohydrate and are not expected to materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × net carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 17.8) / 100 ≈ 12.8, where net carbohydrate per 350 g serving = (25.19 − 3.7) × 3.5 × 0.8 ≈ 17.8 g (adjusted for mixed dish composition). This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for clinical use.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from abalone sauce and oyster sauce; listed 231 mg/100 g likely underestimates actual dish sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to the very high sodium content of abalone sauce (typically 800–1200 mg/100 g) and oyster sauce; if consumed, request minimal sauce and verify actual sodium with the vendor, targeting no more than 600 mg sodium per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base with net carbohydrate of approximately 21.5 g/100 g; abalone sauce contains added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to reduce glycaemic load, request substitution of white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice, and be aware that abalone sauce adds further sugar, collectively raising postprandial blood glucose risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Mixed seafood provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids, but high sodium from sauces elevates cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The mixed seafood component (abalone, scallops, prawns, fish) provides lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for cardiovascular health; however, the high sodium load from abalone and oyster sauces poses a significant cardiovascular risk and sauce quantity should be minimised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3-rich seafood is anti-inflammatory, but high sauce sodium may exacerbate inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mixed seafood provides omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein with anti-inflammatory properties beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis; however, the high sodium content of abalone and oyster sauces should be minimised, targeting below 400 mg sodium per 100 g of the full dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Abalone Sauce Seafood Rice (鲍汁海鲜捞饭, Bào Zhī Hǎixiān Lāo Fàn) is a Chinese-Singaporean rice dish consisting of a base of steamed white jasmine rice topped with a medley of mixed seafood — typically abalone, scallops, prawns, and fish — dressed in a rich, savoury abalone sauce often combined with oyster sauce, ginger, and garlic. The dish is a popular lunch and dinner option in Singapore's Chinese restaurants and hawker-adjacent eateries. The texture contrasts soft, slightly sticky rice with tender seafood and a glossy, umami-forward sauce. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein (6.5 g/100 g) from the seafood components, moderate fat (4.21 g/100 g), and a significant carbohydrate load (25.19 g/100 g) from the white rice base. Dietary fibre is relatively high at 3.7 g/100 g, likely from vegetables and the rice itself. The listed sodium of 231 mg/100 g is considered a likely underestimate given that abalone sauce and oyster sauce are extremely high-sodium condiments (800–1200 mg/100 g individually). The dish carries a high glycaemic index (~72) due to the white rice base, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Iron content is notable at 2.55 mg/100 g, contributed by the seafood. Potassium (327 mg/100 g) and calcium (54 mg/100 g) are also present. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for hypertension and type 2 diabetes without significant modification, but the seafood component provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 benefits relevant to cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis management.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010916), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The match is exact. GI is an analogous estimate based on the white rice component from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source; this does not reduce overall confidence to medium as the nutrient data itself is exact.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010916) as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as reported by HPB (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo G00010916); this is plausible for a mixed seafood and rice dish where the HPB analytical method may have yielded a below-detection result. Sodium listed at 231 mg/100 g is likely a significant underestimate of the actual dish sodium when abalone sauce and oyster sauce are applied in typical restaurant quantities; clinical guidance reflects this caveat. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white jasmine rice (University of Sydney GI Database); direct GI measurement of this composite dish is not available in any approved source. Halal status is unconfirmed as abalone sauce and oyster sauce formulations vary by brand and may contain non-halal additives.
